data.title: SpaceX IPO Rewrites The Playbook For Rocket Lab
data.description:
I am more bullish now on Rocket Lab (RKLB) because the SpaceX (SPACE) IPO is likely to reframe the entire sector around platform economics rather than pure launch metrics, and Rocket Lab is already moving in that direction. Given its over $2 billion in backlog that is mostly associated with defense projects, Rocket Lab has visibility others can’t compete with.

In addition, vertical integration and more involvement in Space Systems makes Rocket Lab a closer match for becoming a comprehensive space infrastructure company. With SpaceX setting the bar in terms of platform valuations, RKLB is well-positioned to gain from this, while Neutron represents the next value-driver.

SpaceX IPO Changes the Game for Space Stocks

In my opinion, the SpaceX IPO process, as such, is not about SpaceX itself, but rather about how this company transforms the whole space investment landscape. The first important takeaway from the Analyst Day was the fact that the company chose an almost three-day-long institutional tour which involved visiting Starbase and the Memphis "Macrohard" data center, which signals its shift toward becoming infrastructure instead of aerospace.

The fact that the core asset is Starlink should be noted separately. In particular, the $11.4 billion revenue and 63% EBITDA margins, along with 10 million+ users make this product similar to a telecom service or platform rather than a regular space company. In my opinion, it has tremendous implications for the public markets' approach to valuing the industry.

Of course, I should not overlook the impact that the $4.94 billion xAI investment-driven loss will have on the valuation story. I believe that it emphasizes the idea that SpaceX is evolving into a capital-intensive platform. For Rocket Lab, this IPO will be an important validation event. At the same time it is clear that this will be a relatively positioning event as well which means that money will continue flowing into the industry at the same time, it will become increasingly selective.

The IPO Sparks a Capital Surge and Rocket Lab Stands First in Line

What I found most interesting about the framing of the Netscape moment was not the comparison but the rationale behind it. With the creation of the first benchmark that can be considered institutionally relevant within a sector, investors don't only pour money into that benchmark but also begin to make allocations towards the entire class of assets based on the new reference point.

In the case of SpaceX going public with a $1.75 trillion valuation, there is going to be increased demand for an insight into the asset class of space travel. The reality, however, is that they aren't going to see enough allocation in SpaceX. This leads to another level, where capital starts looking for a proxy in terms of liquid investments that offer the ability to execute the mission. To me, it seems that Rocket Lab finds itself in such an advantageous position in terms of the new benchmark.

Valuation re-anchoring cannot be overlooked either. With the company trading at revenue multiples in the triple digits due to platform economics, the market will likely re-evaluate how the rest of the vertically integrated space companies are valued, including Rocket Lab's.

However, I do not see it as a free ride despite the halo effect that is created through the IPO process. Capital is going to become even more discerning, and Rocket Lab needs to prove that it deserves to be the public benchmark.

A Premium That Reflects Transition

Where things get tricky for me is in the nature of Rocket Lab as an asset class. In valuing them as a launch provider, the multiples look very rich. However, once you start to view RKLB as a vertically integrated space infrastructure player, everything changes.

At ~60x forward sales for 2026 before compressing to ~20x by 2030 with consensus revenue scaling to ~$2.5 billion the market is certainly betting on execution and time. This is not a quick-turn story but rather a multiyear transition story, one which requires revenue, margin, and business mix all trending in the right direction.

While that may sound optimistic, the key difference from other early stage multiples is that certain pieces of the story are already visible. For example, backlog gives near term revenue visibility, Space Systems drives mix improvement and gross margins are now expanding into the mid-30s.

For me, the multiple becomes far less relevant than the trajectory of that multiple. Specifically, as long as Rocket Lab hits revenue expectations for 2027/2028 at the $1.2-$1.5 billion level while growing margins, the multiple should naturally fall as part of this growth narrative.

One thing that makes things difficult is the recent SpaceX IPO. If SpaceX comes to market with a multiple around ~125x revenues and sells the story as an infrastructure story, there's no doubt that it will set new boundaries for the way that the market prices space infrastructure plays.

Ultimately, Rocket Lab doesn't need to come in at the same multiple but it does need to be seen as playing in the same universe. The problem of course is that any issues with these assumptions could disrupt the revenue growth and hence the multiple entirely. In sum, I'd say that Rocket Lab remains an aggressive but increasingly justifiable valuation story as long as the narrative holds up.

Risks: The SpaceX Halo Trade Cuts Both Ways

While the hype cycle is certainly a concern regarding the SpaceX IPO, the second-order effects that result from such an event are what should worry me more. Although the influx of cash flow is significant, the issue is that while it can help the entire sector, it can also lead to a consolidation of differentiation.

For example, if SpaceX is able to get listed under a very aggressive valuation and capture the institutional capital there is a scenario where SpaceX could take significant space exposure money. In this case, Rocket Lab will have missed out on the expected gains and become a second pick rather than a first pick for investment purposes. Although this is less likely, it should be kept in mind nonetheless.

A possible risk factor can also include the scenario where we see the "sell the news" phenomenon. Rocket Lab has seen a strong rally before the release of this news; therefore, once they hit their high at this news release time, it may not follow through on anything else after. Ultimately, this will result in a retracement.

However, the real risk is that once SpaceX sets an example of platform economics, Rocket Lab will need to step up its game. For instance, if Neutron continues to slip, the company will not be considered an emerging platform anymore but simply one not quite ready for prime time.

Bottom Line

To sum up, Rocket Lab looks like a more mature player today as its strategic orientation shifts to building a vertically integrated space platform. At the same time, its financial performance looks increasingly interesting. Therefore, despite its high valuations, I consider RKLB to be an attractive investment opportunity. While I will continue monitoring progress of the company, I do not see any reasons for selling my shares at this point.

data.title: Business Conditions Monthly February 2026
data.description:
By Peter C. Earle

The February 2026 AIER Business Conditions Monthly (BCM) highlights a notable divergence across the economic cycle, with forward-looking indicators softening, contemporaneous measures deteriorating, and lagging data continuing to reflect earlier resilience, although some of this pattern may be influenced by incomplete data and ongoing catch-up from prior reporting gaps.

Leading Indicator (46)

The Leading Indicator registered 46, with five of 12 components improving, one unchanged, and six declining.

Advances were concentrated in a handful of forward-looking and demand-related measures. The 1-Year to 10-Year US Treasury Yield Spread narrowed sharply by 40.4 percent but was scored positively given its inversion. Labor market forward conditions strengthened as US Initial Jobless Claims SA declined 7.0 percent (a positive after inversion). Adjusted Retail and Food Services Sales Total SA increased 0.7 percent, while the Conference Board US Manufacturers New Orders Nondefense Capital Goods Ex Aircraft rose 0.5 percent. The Conference Board US Leading Index Manufacturers’ New Orders Consumer Goods and Materials edged higher by 0.1 percent.

These gains were outweighed by declines across several key areas. The Conference Board US Leading Index Stock Prices 500 Common Stocks fell 0.6 percent, and the University of Michigan Consumer Expectations Index declined 0.7 percent. The Inventory-to-Sales Ratio Total Business dropped 1.5 percent, and Debit Balances in Customers’ Securities Margin Accounts decreased 2.0 percent. United States Heavy Truck Sales SAAR fell 3.3 percent, while US New Privately Owned Housing Units Started by Structure Total SAAR declined 3.4 percent. US Average Weekly Hours All Employees Manufacturing SA was unchanged.

Taken together, the leading components point to a loss of momentum in forward indicators, with isolated areas of strength unable to offset broader softness in housing, expectations, and financial market signals.

Roughly Coincident Indicator (17)

The Roughly Coincident Indicator came in at 17, with one component improving and five declining.

US Industrial Production increased 0.7 percent, representing the sole area of strength. Elsewhere, conditions weakened: Conference Board Coincident Manufacturing and Trade Sales slipped 0.1 percent, while US Employees on Nonfarm Payrolls Total SA was essentially flat, posting a slight decline. US Labor Force Participation Rate edged down 0.2 percent, and Conference Board Coincident Personal Income Less Transfer Payments fell 0.4 percent. Conference Board Consumer Confidence Present Situation SA dropped 2.5 percent.

Overall, the coincident data depict a soft and weakening current environment, where declines in income, participation, and sentiment outweigh modest gains in production.

Lagging Indicator (67)

The Lagging Indicator stood at 67, with four components improving and two declining.

Strength was concentrated in credit and inventory measures. Conference Board US Lagging Commercial and Industrial Loans rose 2.1 percent, while US Commercial Paper Placed Top 30 Day Yield increased 1.1 percent. US Manufacturing and Trade Inventories Total SA advanced 0.4 percent, and Census Bureau US Private Construction Spending Nonresidential SA increased 0.2 percent. Against this, US CPI Urban Consumers Less Food and Energy Year over Year NSA declined 1.9 percent, and the Conference Board US Lagging Average Duration of Unemployment rose 8.4 percent and was scored negatively after inversion.

The lagging components continue to reflect underlying firmness in credit conditions and inventories, though the increase in unemployment duration and easing inflation suggest that slack is beginning to emerge beneath the surface.

The February 2026 BCM readings point to a deterioration in forward and contemporaneous conditions alongside continued firmness in trailing measures. The Leading Indicator (46) signals fading momentum, with gains in select demand indicators overshadowed by declines in housing, expectations, and market-based measures. The Roughly Coincident Indicator (17) underscores a weak present, marked by broad-based softness in income, participation, and sentiment. By contrast, the Lagging Indicator (67) reflects residual strength in credit and inventory accumulation, even as labor market strains begin to build. Compared with January’s configuration (63, 42, 33), the shift is notable: forward and current indicators have weakened materially, while lagging measures have strengthened, consistent with a loss of economic momentum. Taken together, the pattern suggests an economy transitioning from uneven expansion toward a more fragile footing.

As with the prior release, these figures should be interpreted with caution, as the data continue to fill in following recent gaps and may not yet provide a fully reliable picture of underlying trends.

Discussion, March–April 2026

March’s CPI report reflects a sharp but narrowly driven acceleration in headline inflation, with gasoline prices — surging in the wake of the Iran conflict — accounting for the bulk of the increase. Headline CPI rose 0.87 percent month-over-month, the fastest pace in nearly four years, lifting the year-over-year rate to 3.3 percent from 2.4 percent. In contrast, core CPI remained subdued at 0.20 percent on the month and 2.6 percent year-over-year, as easing in services and stability across several heavily weighted goods categories helped contain broader price pressures. Energy alone contributed roughly 70 basis points to the monthly headline gain, with gasoline prices jumping over 21 percent on a seasonally adjusted basis — the largest increase on record. Outside of energy and a modest pickup in airfares, the transmission of higher commodity prices into consumer prices remains limited for now, reflecting the typical lag in pass-through. Food prices were largely flat, while core goods inflation held at 0.1 percent amid a mix of declines in categories such as used cars, household furnishings, and prescription drugs, offset by strength in technology-related goods tied to memory chip shortages and selective increases in apparel and recreation items.

Beneath the surface, inflation dynamics remain mixed but relatively contained, with some evidence of modest firming in the breadth of price increases alongside continued softness in key service categories. The share of core components running above the Fed’s two percent target edged higher, though still below levels seen in prior years, while core services inflation slowed to 0.2 percent despite a slight reacceleration in rents. Discretionary services — including travel, lodging, and recreation — showed signs of weakening, potentially reflecting early consumer sensitivity to higher fuel costs. Looking ahead, near-term inflation is likely to remain elevated as energy prices continue to feed through, with additional pressure expected in airfares and a possible one-time firming in rents. However, a recently announced ceasefire and still-muted core trends suggest that underlying inflation may remain manageable. Against this backdrop, the Federal Reserve is likely to maintain a wait-and-see posture, holding rates steady through much of 2026 as it assesses the balance between transient energy-driven inflation and a still-cooling core, with markets continuing to price limited near-term policy easing.

Complementing this picture, February’s PCE data indicate that underlying inflation pressures were already firming before the Iran conflict, particularly within goods categories tied to global supply chains. Core PCE — the Fed’s preferred gauge — remained elevated near 3 percent year-over-year, with shorter-run annualized measures moving higher, driven in part by durable goods such as vehicles, apparel, and technology-related components linked to memory chip shortages. At the same time, service sector inflation showed signs of moderation, with categories such as health care and recreation contributing less to overall price growth. Notably, core PCE appears to have accelerated relative to core CPI, reversing its typical relationship and widening the gap between the two measures to unusually large levels. This divergence reflects differences in composition and weighting — particularly PCE’s greater exposure to financial services, food services, and technology-related goods — while also highlighting how sector-specific shocks can shape broader inflation readings.

Taken together, the data suggest an inflation environment that is neither reaccelerating broadly nor decisively cooling but instead being shaped by a shifting mix of supply-side pressures and uneven demand. Energy- and goods-related shocks are pushing headline measures higher and adding volatility to core readings, while services — still the dominant driver of inflation — are gradually easing but remain firm enough to prevent a rapid return to target. As of early April 2026, U.S. inflation appears increasingly bifurcated: headline measures are being driven by geopolitical and commodity shocks, while underlying inflation is moderating only slowly and unevenly. This leaves the overall trajectory uncertain, with inflation neither clearly reaccelerating nor convincingly returning to the Federal Reserve’s two percent objective.

Recent labor market data present a picture of resilience in the near term, though much of the apparent strength reflects temporary factors rather than a clear reacceleration in underlying demand. March payrolls rebounded by 178,000 following a weather- and strike-depressed February, with gains concentrated in sectors that had previously been disrupted, including leisure and hospitality, construction, and health care. The resolution of the Kaiser Permanente strike alone mechanically boosted employment, while improved weather conditions supported hiring in cyclical sectors. Private payrolls rose 186,000, led by services, though pockets of weakness persisted in professional and business services, finance, and information — areas likely facing structural adjustments, including ongoing layoffs tied to technological change. Wage growth moderated to 0.2 percent, and a slight decline in hours worked weighed on aggregate income, suggesting that labor market momentum remains modest beneath the headline rebound. The unemployment rate fell to 4.26 percent, though this was driven in part by a decline in labor force participation, pointing to a labor market that is stable but not tightening materially.

Broader indicators continue to signal a gradual cooling in labor demand, reinforcing the view that conditions are settling rather than strengthening. Job openings declined to 6.88 million in February, while the hiring rate fell to its lowest level since 2020, reflecting employer caution amid rising costs and growing uncertainty even before the escalation of the Iran conflict. The ratio of vacancies to unemployed workers remains below one, indicating that labor supply is no longer being outpaced by demand, while the quits rate has fallen to pandemic-era lows, suggesting reduced worker confidence in job mobility. Initial jobless claims have edged higher but remain historically contained and geographically limited, underscoring the absence of broad-based layoffs. Taken together, the data suggest a labor market that is holding up in the short run — supported by temporary rebounds and seasonal factors — but gradually losing dynamism. Looking ahead, higher input costs and tighter financial conditions could weigh more heavily on hiring in the second half of the year, leaving policymakers inclined to remain patient as they assess whether current stability gives way to more meaningful softening.

March’s ISM Manufacturing report suggests continued expansion, though the underlying details point to a more nuanced and less robust picture than the headline implies. The PMI rose to 52.7, supported largely by slower supplier deliveries and inventory drawdowns tied to supply disruptions stemming from the Iran conflict, rather than a broad-based strengthening in demand. In contrast, forward-looking components softened: new orders eased to 53.5, export demand declined, and order backlogs grew more slowly, indicating some loss of momentum on the demand side. Production picked up, and inventories were depleted more quickly, consistent with firms working through constrained supply chains rather than responding to accelerating end demand. At the same time, the prices paid index surged to 78.3, reflecting a sharp rise in input costs, while employment continued to contract modestly. Taken together, the report points to an industrial sector still expanding but increasingly shaped by the tension between rising costs and moderating demand, with supply-side disruptions playing an outsized role in recent strength.

The ISM Services report, by contrast, highlights continued growth but with clearer signs of strain from rising costs and operational pressures. The headline index declined to 54.0 from 56.1, remaining in expansion territory but marking a loss of momentum, even as new orders strengthened further and export demand improved. Beneath the surface, however, firms are facing a sharp increase in input costs, with the prices index jumping to 70.7 — the largest monthly gain in nearly 14 years — and supply chains showing renewed signs of stress. These pressures appear to be weighing on hiring, with the employment component falling into contraction for the first time in several months and production slowing. Business commentary points to rising fuel costs, logistical disruptions, and broader geopolitical uncertainty as key challenges, particularly in transportation and travel-related industries. Overall, the services sector remains a source of growth, but the combination of firm demand and intensifying cost pressures introduces a more balanced and uncertain outlook, especially as firms adjust hiring and investment decisions in response to the evolving environment.

Recent sentiment data across consumers and small businesses suggest a modest improvement in current conditions but a growing sense of caution about the outlook, particularly as rising energy costs and geopolitical uncertainty begin to weigh on expectations. The Conference Board’s consumer confidence index edged higher in March, driven by a stronger assessment of present conditions and a still-stable view of the labor market, with slightly more consumers reporting jobs as plentiful. However, forward-looking components weakened, including expectations for income, employment, and spending across services categories. At the same time, inflation expectations moved sharply higher, with year-ahead expectations rising above 6 percent, signaling that higher oil prices are beginning to shape household perceptions. The University of Michigan survey paints a somewhat softer picture overall, with headline sentiment declining and both current conditions and expectations deteriorating, particularly after the onset of the Iran conflict. While short-term inflation expectations rose, longer-term expectations remained relatively anchored, suggesting consumers still view the current price pressures as at least partially transitory.

Small business sentiment also softened meaningfully in March, reflecting rising costs and elevated uncertainty, though the deterioration remains concentrated in expectations rather than current activity. The NFIB Small Business Optimism Index fell below its long-run average for the first time in nearly a year, driven by weaker profit trends, declining expectations for business conditions, and reduced plans for capital spending. At the same time, hiring plans held steady, and expected real sales eased only modestly, indicating that firms have yet to materially pull back on operations. The sharp rise in the uncertainty index underscores the role of policy, cost pressures, and geopolitical developments in shaping business outlooks. Taken together, the data suggest a sentiment environment that is holding up at present but becoming increasingly fragile, with rising inflation expectations and uncertainty posing risks to both consumer spending and business investment in the months ahead.

Recent retail and consumption data point to a consumer sector that remains nominally resilient but is showing clearer signs of strain once adjusted for inflation and income dynamics. Headline retail sales rose a strong 1.7 percent in March, boosted in large part by higher gasoline prices, with gains broadly distributed across categories including furniture, general merchandise, and online spending. Even excluding autos and gas, sales increased a solid 0.6 percent, and control group sales — a key input into GDP — rose 0.7 percent. However, much of this strength appears to reflect price effects and temporary supports such as tax refunds and higher-income spending, rather than a sustained acceleration in real demand. In real terms, control group sales were likely flat, consistent with a slowdown in first-quarter consumption growth to around 1.0 percent from 1.9 percent previously. Earlier PCE data reinforce this softer underlying picture: real personal spending rose just 0.1 percent in February, with services — typically the more stable component — slowing to its weakest pace in several months, while goods spending was constrained by rising prices in categories such as vehicles and technology-related items.

At the same time, income growth has softened, adding to pressure on household finances. Personal income declined modestly in February, with slower gains in compensation and declines in transfer payments and dividend income weighing on the headline. With spending continuing to outpace income, the saving rate fell to 4.0 percent, while real disposable income growth has slowed to one of its weakest rates in recent years. This combination suggests that consumers are increasingly relying on reduced savings and selective spending adjustments to maintain consumption levels. Evidence of this adjustment is already visible in weaker discretionary services spending and in goods categories where higher prices appear to be dampening volumes. Taken together, the data suggest that while consumer spending has held up in nominal terms, underlying real demand is softening, leaving consumption increasingly vulnerable to further increases in energy prices and broader cost pressures.

Recent data on business investment and production suggest that underlying industrial momentum was solid heading into the Iran conflict, though near-term readings have been distorted by sector-specific swings and early supply disruptions. February’s durable goods report showed a headline decline of 1.4 percent driven largely by a sharp drop in aircraft orders, but underlying demand was firm, with orders excluding transportation rising 0.8 percent and broad-based gains across primary metals, machinery, and motor vehicles. Core capital goods orders and shipments — key indicators of equipment investment — both strengthened, pointing to a healthy pace of business spending prior to the escalation in geopolitical tensions. By contrast, March industrial production fell 0.5 percent, reflecting a combination of payback from strong February revisions, weaker vehicle output, declining utilities production, and emerging supply chain constraints tied to the conflict. Manufacturing output edged lower despite prior signs of strength in survey data, while capacity utilization slipped modestly. Taken together, the data suggest that the investment cycle entered the current period on relatively firm footing, but faces a more uncertain outlook as defense-related demand and supply disruptions offset potentially softer private sector activity amid rising costs and heightened uncertainty.

Pulling back to a wider view, recent macro data suggest the U.S. economy entered 2026 with firmer underlying momentum than headline figures imply, even as the current environment has become more uncertain. Fourth-quarter GDP was revised down to a modest 0.5 percent growth rate, but income-based measures point to stronger activity, with real gross domestic income rising 2.6 percent and corporate profits posting robust gains. Much of this divergence reflects technical distortions tied to last fall’s government shutdown, which depressed measured output while leaving underlying income and demand comparatively intact; real final sales to private domestic purchasers still advanced a solid 1.8 percent. This stronger foundation is broadly consistent with the Federal Reserve’s latest Beige Book, which describes continued, if modest, expansion across most regions through early April, with consumer spending holding up and manufacturing activity generally improving. At the same time, the report highlights a shift in tone, with firms increasingly citing geopolitical tensions and rising costs as sources of uncertainty, leading many to adopt a more cautious, wait-and-see approach to hiring, pricing, and investment decisions.

Against that backdrop, the policy outlook has tilted more cautious and incrementally hawkish, with the Federal Reserve signaling a clear preference to remain on hold amid heightened uncertainty and persistent inflation risks. Minutes from the March FOMC meeting indicate that while officials still see a path to eventual rate cuts, that timing has been pushed further out, with greater concern that inflation may prove more durable — even raising the possibility of additional tightening if price pressures fail to ease. At the same time, policymakers recognize a two-sided risk environment in which the same oil-driven shock that lifts inflation could ultimately weigh on employment and growth, reinforcing a data-dependent and “nimble” approach. Near-term data are expected to show continued firm activity alongside elevated cost pressures, though the partial easing in oil prices following the early-April ceasefire may help reduce immediate risks to both inflation and growth. Fiscal factors are also providing some near-term support to demand, with tax refunds helping to sustain consumption despite rising energy costs. Overall, the policy stance reflects a balancing act: holding steady in the face of competing risks while deferring any easing until clearer evidence emerges that inflation is durably moving back toward target.

Stepping back, the broader picture is one of an economy that remains fundamentally intact and, in several respects, more resilient than headline volatility might suggest. Growth entered 2026 on solid footing, inflation — while temporarily elevated by energy — remains contained beneath the surface, and both labor markets and business activity continue to expand, albeit at a more measured pace. At the same time, the composition of recent data reveals a system under pressure rather than in decline: real consumption is softening, hiring is becoming more selective, and firms are navigating rising input costs and supply disruptions with caution rather than retrenchment. The outlook, therefore, is cautiously constructive — supported by stable income growth, still-positive demand, and the likelihood that current inflation shocks will fade — but increasingly clouded by policy ambiguity, lingering tariff risks, and the unpredictable path of the Iran conflict. These crosscurrents leave the economy in a delicate but not unfavorable position: resilient in the present, but with confidence and momentum vulnerable to further shocks or missteps in policy.

data.title: New Oriental Education: Above-Expectations Performance And Outlook
data.description:
I've kept New Oriental Education (EDU) (9901.HK) as a Buy-rated name.

Both the company's 3QFY26 (YE May) results and Q4 guidance were superior than what the market hoped for. Its shares are also attractively-priced based on a peer comparison.

The earlier January 30, 2026 write-up highlighted my constructive view of its second quarter 'beat-and-raise'.

Results Topped Expectations

The group published a 6-K revealing its recent financials on April 22.

EDU's topline expansion improved from 2QFY2026's +14.7% to 3QFY2026's +19.8% on a YoY basis. Its normalized EPS was also 37% higher than the same quarter a year ago.

I'm impressed with the firm's better-than-anticipated showing. The $1.42B turnover and $0.95/ADS bottom-line for the latest three-month period beat the consensus projections by 4.3% and 12.6%, respectively.

My take is that increased wallet share and a leaner operational model boosted EDU's quarterly numbers.

At the analyst briefing, the company indicated that "paid users pay more money and enroll more subjects at the same time." That drove up its combined "nonacademic tutoring and learning device" revenues by 23% year-on-year in 3QFY26. I think it has found success in cross-selling different educational solutions to students.

Also, its EBIT-to-sales widened 2.3ppts YoY to 14.3% for the Dec '25 to Feb '26 quarter. That's 10bps above the sell-side's prediction as per S&P Capital IQ.

The enterprise lowered its OPEX-to-revenue by 360bps between 3QFY2025 and 3QFY2026 due to disciplined footprint extension. Its call commentary mentioned that the "net addition of the new learning centers in first 3 quarters was 8%." This is less aggressive than the original low-to-mid teens goal.

Anticipating A Similar High-Teens Growth Next Quarter

The mid-point of EDU's 4QFY2026 top-line guide is $1,448.3M, implying a 17% rise. According to S&P Capital IQ, analysts were previously forecasting a more modest $1.43B.

In my opinion, the company's March-May '26 performance will be driven by higher throughput and more effective customer acquisition.

EDU emphasized at the results meeting that its "utilization rate will continuously go up going forward." It outlined the approach of placing more "new student enrollments into the existing learning centers." I believe this should translate into favorable fixed-cost leverage that impacts its future profitability positively.

I see its advertising and promotion expenses trending downwards as well. Management's briefing remarks suggested that "we don't need to spend crazy money on marketing to recruit the new student." It attributed the mounting confidence to a "very healthy growth of the K-12 business."

I expect EDU's revenue momentum to be sustained in the near-term, considering these above-mentioned levers.

Mid-Term Prospects Are Bolstered By AI-Related Measures And Refreshed Positioning

I'm encouraged by the firm's efforts to maximize the benefits brought about by technological advancements. EDU's Q3 call outlined new AI-driven products replicating "offline face-to-face teaching" and plans for a more intensive "use of AI to streamline internal operations."

I can imagine the group generating incremental sales from digital learning and becoming leaner with a reduced reliance on human labor in the future.

Separately, EDU has adopted a new family-centric strategy by launching a platform called "New Oriental Home/NOH."

It wants to broaden its reach beyond students to couples and elders. The company also runs a live-streaming e-commerce unit called "East Buy" and an organized tour management division. It hopes to unlock synergies across its various units to target a wider demographic.

EDU's aim is to cross-market all its offerings as a bundle going forward. The early traction looks promising. NOH has already signed up over 300k families. The lead-to-conversion yield for initial marketing efforts was in the double-digits percentage range.

I'm optimistic about NOH's runway. It's only available in 12 Chinese cities now. The company's core education business has a more substantial presence spanning 60 local municipalities.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Variant Views

A faster-than-anticipated pace of investments may hurt EDU's short-term margins. I assume that the new NOH initiative and AI solutions will take some time to scale up.

The recent actions taken to moderate capacity increases and marketing expenditures could lead to market share loss. As EDU places a greater emphasis on profitability for the near future, its rivals might take the opportunity to "steal" its potential clients.

My Closing Thoughts

I'm still positive on the stock.

The market is currently valuing EDU at a forward FY2026 P/E of 15.3x (source: S&P Capital IQ).

Its domestic peer TAL Education Group trades at a more demanding 18.3 times. New Oriental's capital appreciation potential is +20% (18.3/15.3) based on my benchmarking exercise. This justifies a 'Buy rating' for this name.

I've already pinpointed several re-rating drivers in my article. One of them is a similarly strong rate of revenue expansion in Q4. Also, investors should be pleased if EDU can utilize AI more effectively to improve its future results. The "New Oriental Home" bundling strategy is only in the early stages of implementation, implying huge upside.

data.title: Coinbase: Bitcoin's Rising Tide Masks A Retail Moat In Structural Decline
data.description:
Coinbase (COIN) has historically been one of the few regulated and publicly listed vehicles through which institutional investors could gain exposure to the crypto space, apart from investing in Bitcoin (BTC) itself. Today, there are many other solid ways to get crypto exposure (even in size), so the appeal via scarcity has diminished. By investing in COIN, not only are we looking to gain exposure to crypto but also through a financial infrastructure intermediary company, which may often be an inefficient method to capture alpha in the underlying sector. Below, we can observe that the stock trades with a high correlation to crypto prices—Bitcoin in particular. However, since the inception of iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) in January 2024, COIN has in fact underperformed holding BTC via the ETF.

The business model

Coinbase operates through two main segments—transaction revenue, and subscription and services revenue.

1. Transaction revenue accounted for $4.1B or ~56%, of total revenue in FY2025. Within this segment, Consumer contributed the vast majority (~82%), while Institutional and other transaction revenue were ~12% and ~6% of segment revenue, respectively.

2. Subscription and services revenue accounted for $2.8B or ~39%, of total revenue in FY2025. Stablecoin revenue made up the bulk of this segment at ~48%, followed by blockchain rewards at ~24%, and the rest, which included interest and finance fee income and other subscription and services revenue, added up to ~28% of segment revenue.

Overall, FY 2025 total revenue reached $7.2B, growing 9% Y/Y, while adjusted EPS jumped 24.6% Y/Y to $2.19.

A two-year lesson in capital misallocation

Suppose an investor was seeking crypto exposure and was making an allocation choice in January 2024 when the iShares Bitcoin Trust was launched. Investing in IBIT would have yielded a total return of 59.6% (or 22.83% average annual return), while COIN would only have returned 38.8% (or 15.51% average annual return), losing out to SPY's 51.9% total return.

This is a meaningful underperformance as investing in COIN would neither have fully captured equity growth nor the crypto beta cleanly. Instead, the investor would have been exposed to the jarring volatility associated with crypto assets while being in a margin compressed and regulated financial intermediary.

The stock jumped on the inclusion into the S&P 500 in May 2025, but it proved to be fleeting as it now trades back to similar levels.

Tethered to the cyclicality of crypto volumes

As depicted in the chart below, crypto trading volume tends to follow closely with prices—peak volume in October 2025 coincided with peak prices in Bitcoin in the same month. Volume has since faltered, while Bitcoin has corrected nearly 40% from $126K.

Coinbase relies mainly on consumer/retail transactions for fee generation, which carry significantly higher fee rates (up to 60bps). We believe fee compression is a general trend in most financial markets, and because competitors overall already have much lower blended fee rates, we see little reason for Coinbase to not lower their charges to match peers in the future.

According to data from The Block, Binance still facilitates the lion's share of crypto trading volume, with ~30% of total volume in March being traded there. Bybit is second with ~7% share, while Coinbase is third at ~6% share.

Coinbase's monthly market share has been mostly stable around ~5%–~6%, while other smaller platforms in aggregate have eroded Binance's market share from high 30s to 30.

The rise in popularity of DEX

A notable example would be the arrival of Hyperliquid since launching in 2023. By 2025, it had already surpassed Coinbase in notional trading volume for perpetual contracts. In 2025, it recorded $2.6T in notional trading volume, vastly superior to Coinbase's $1.4T. Total perpetuals volume reached $7.9T in 2025.

According to The Block, Hyperliquid handles >6% of total exchange as of April and is on a rising trend. Year-to-date, Hyperliquid has driven $0.77T in cumulative perpetual volume, or $2.56T if annualized.

The strategic response from Coinbase was its acquisition of a large centralized options trading platform, Deribit, in August 2025. Deribit processed over 80% of total Bitcoin options volume in October 2025, while the average volume share is around 70%.

Deribit competes in the options segment, which is predominantly used by more sophisticated institutional clients as opposed to retail traders who prefer directional, leveraged trading in perpetuals. So, this acquisition is unlikely to resolve the disruption brought by DEX.

Stablecoin growth still lacks clarity

Coinbase's stablecoin revenue mainly rests on the USDC adoption. While being the largest regulated USD-denominated stablecoin in circulation, USDC has yet to make enough headway to dethrone its much larger brethren, USDT. According to DefiLlama, USDT in circulation is $188.5B (~70% market share) versus USDC's $77.9B (~29% market share), as of April 22.

Despite the regulatory tailwind, USDT still has its first-mover and liquidity advantage, particularly driven by demand across Asia and Africa.

The recently advanced Digital Asset Market Clarity Act has yet to provide enough clarity on stablecoin law, especially when it comes to yield. As of now, issuers like Circle are prohibited from paying USDC holders any interest. But, providing incentives linked to digital asset activity appears to be the workaround for now. There is still ongoing debate as to whether such rewards should be classified as interest.

But for Coinbase, there is meaningful revenue implication. Stablecoin revenue rose 48% Y/Y in FY2025 and relies on USDC adoption to be successful. Without offering interest or incentives for holding or using USDC, adoption could falter and directly impact the company's revenues.

Blockchain rewards facing compression

Participation in the blockchain often rewards staking yield, which has helped contribute $677M in revenue for Coinbase in FY2025, down 4% Y/Y.

Most of the staking yield should come from Ethereum, which has generally offered low to mid single-digit yields post-Merge. Currently, ETH staking yield sits at ~2.9%, according to Staking Rewards. Similarly, the BlackRock ETHB staking ETF offers a 2.8% yield, as of February 18. The yield can fluctuate depending on network activity and, most importantly, the staking participation rate.

Liquid restaking introduces enhancements to plain-vanilla ETH staking by offering layered yield strategies. These strategies can carry additional risk but can offer varying degrees of incremental yield. Sophisticated users may opt to move away from Coinbase's staking platform to self-directed LRT protocols in search of higher yield. However, we have observed that yield spreads on LRTs have materially declined to ~1%.

Declining operating leverage

Total revenue in 4Q25 fell 22.2% Y/Y, while transaction revenue dropped 36.8% Y/Y to $982.7M. But operating expenses rose 41% Y/Y to $1.3B as headcount grew 3% Y/Y on top of surging SG&A and R&D expenses.

The market predicts capex to reach $144M in FY2026E and rise to $159M in FY2027E, from virtually zero in FY2024 as a result of the Deribit acquisition and infrastructure investment. This should weigh on FCF in the coming years.

Valuation and verdict

If we look towards the outer years in FY2027E/FY2028E, both revenue and EPS noticeably improve. The market is discounting FY2026E with modest declines in revenue and a larger drop in EPS due to reverse operating leverage. The year has started lackluster in 2026, correcting from a peak in October 2025. But crypto assets have meaningfully rebounded from March lows, helped by fresh highs in U.S. equities and optimism from a potential resolution of the U.S.-Iran conflict.

That said, the stock trades at an FY2027E P/E of 38x and an FY2027E EV/EBITDA of 13.9x. Compared with its broker peers, IBKR trades at 27.7x FY2027E P/E, while HOOD trades at 32x FY2027E P/E. Coinbase is more richly valued, but this is a premium that is increasingly difficult to justify.

While COIN has built a strong moat around regulated crypto dealing, its retail business, from which it makes most of its profit, has come under intense competition. On the other hand, we believe its institutional franchise is a bright spot where the company serves as a crypto asset custodian for many institutional products.

On balance, we think COIN is a Hold until valuations or fundamentals improve. In the near term, it is likely to benefit from rebounding crypto assets.

data.title: GE Aerospace: This 10% Dip Looks Like A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Shares of GE Aerospace (NYSE:GE) have lost nearly 10% since my last report in which I pointed out that GE Aerospace remains attractive for the long-term and even with GDP growth risk translating to lower service demand analysts had not substantially altered their near-term expectations. GE’s Q1 earnings showing continued strength, but we note that the economic growth concerns prevented GE Aerospace from raising its full year target. So, we do see that there is not only hesitance among investors but also within the management ranks. That, however, does not weaken my view that GE Aerospace provides a compelling investment opportunity for investors. In this report, I discuss the Q1 2026 earnings and update my price target.

GE Aerospace Delivers A Strong Beat Despite Margin Compression

Revenues rose to $11.6 billion marking 29% growth in revenues. Calling that strong growth is an understatement. Analysts had expected around $10.7 billion in revenues. Operating profit increased 18% and showed margins weakening somewhat two points to 21.8%. Free cash flow grew 14% to $1.7 billion while earnings per share grew 25% to $1.86 and exceeded analyst estimates by $0.26. The margin compression can be attributed to stronger relative growth in lower-margin revenues.

Engine Deliveries Drive Margin Compression For GE Aerospace

First quarter revenues for the Commercial Engines & Services segment rose 34% driven by 39% growth in services revenues and 20% growth in equipment deliveries. Services growth was driven by 35% growth in internal shop visit revenue including a 50% increase in internal shop visits for the CFM LEAP engines powering the Airbus A320neo and Boeing 737 MAX. Equipment sales were driven by an increase of 50% deliveries showing that the mix is leaning towards lower-revenue engines, which is to be expected given that CFM engine deliveries climbed 63%. The operating profit increased 23% to $2.4 billion but margins contracted 2.3 points. This is driven by a higher share of lower margin deliveries to original equipment manufacturers. These deliveries come at zero to low digit margins and drive down the composite margins. Furthermore, GE is investing in the business and while the company stated in their Q1 press release there were no changes on profit estimates on long-term service agreements, they still booked a $30 million charge including a $100 million reversal of tariff related charges recognized in Q1 2025. So, it is not really an absence of charges, but a lower charge setting.

Defense Budgets Are Not Driving Near Term Results

Sales for Defense & Propulsion Technologies grew 19% driven by 14% growth in defense sales consisting of services growth of 9% and equipment sales growth of 31%. Noteworthy is the 2.4x book-to-bill ratio showing that there is strong momentum in defense. Propulsion & Additive Technologies grew by 29% driven by Avio Aero, which designs, manufactures, and maintains civil and military aeronautics engine components and propulsion systems. Avio Aero formerly was part of Avio, which I cover as well, and bought by GE Aerospace in 2012. Operating profit increased 17% to $0.4 billion with margins dropping 20 basis points to 11.8% as volume and price tailwinds were offset by mix, investment and inflation. It does show the inflation risk for GE Aerospace partially.

GE Aerospace Maintains Guidance On Middle East Uncertainty

For 2026, GE Aerospace still targets low double-digit revenue growth driven by mid-teens growth in CES with mid-to-high teens growth in equipment sales and mid-teens growth in services sales. DPT is expected to grow sales by mid-to-high single digits. Operating profit has been guided for at $9.85-$10.25 billion, indicating double-digit growth at the midpoint of the range.

While the guidance has been maintained, GE Aerospace showed an interesting change in its assumptions. Initially, the company had expected high single digit growth for the market in the Middle East. The actual performance in Q1 was a high single digit decline and for the full year a low double-digit decline is now expected. For the full year, all other markets are now targeted at flat to low single digit growth compared to earlier assumptions of low to mid-single digit growth. So, there is some risk on services demand tapering and that is definitely a risk to keep in mind although slowing services revenue growth would come with a lag and would not alter the long-term revenue generation substantially unless we see economic completely fading. That is the main risk for GE Aerospace at this point. The stock markets are reacting positively on ceasefire extensions while the underlying GDP pains are creeping up.

GE Aerospace Stock Has Compelling Upside

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For GE Aerospace, the valuation has become more attractive as the company now shows upside against the peer group EV/EBITDA multiple. This already implies 14% upside with a base case price target of $314.19. That is relatively close to what the company’s premium valuation multiple suggests for this yead and in a more bullish setting the price target would be $370 indicating 34% upside. That is more than enough to maintain my buy rating.

The following changes were implemented to come to the updated price target:

  • A modest lift of 0.3% to the total EBITDA generation between 2026 and 2028.
  • A modest lift of 0.9% to the total free cash flow generation between 2026 and 2028.
  • Increase share repurchases to $9 billion annually after GE disclosed intention (Page 17 of the presentation) to refinance its debt maturities.

For GE Aerospace, the margins are expected to dip this year driven by the ramp up of the GE9X engine deliveries in anticipation of a commercial service entry next year and increases in CFM LEAP engine production. Over time, the margins should grow towards 25%, but this year will see a modest dip. Cash conversion and capital intensity remain balanced with strong growth in free cash flow. As the company signalled intention to refinance its debt, I have modelled share repurchases in that would basically bring the net debt leverage to around 1x. For GE, it does make sense to continue a significant share repurchase program since it has a substantial cash balance while free cash flow keeps growing.

Conclusion: GE Aerospace Faces Some Risk But Remains Attractive For The Long-Term

GE Aerospace is showing some margin pressures, but this is driven by OEM sales increasing and those sales naturally come at lower margins compared to aftermarket sales. We do note that the company kept its guidance unchanged for this year driven by the situation in the Middle East and that does show both the risk for GE and the resilience of the guidance. I maintain a buy rating as I do believe that earnings for 2026 remain strong and over the long-term GE Aerospace has a product portfolio with a multi-decade high-margin revenue tail.

data.title: ServiceNow: Strong Q1, But 52-Week Lows After Hours Offer A Good Entry Point
data.description:
ServiceNow (NOW) delivered a beat on the top line by $20 million, and their EPS came in line with consensus estimates at $0.97. NOW’s subscription revenue came in at $3.77 billion, which increased 22% YoY. NOW beat the high end of every guided metric in Q1, and the stock still dropped by double-digit percentages after hours. NOW guided for $15.74–$15.78 billion of subscription revenue for the 2026 fiscal year, which was an increase of $210 million at the midpoint. Now Assist customers are spending over $1 million in annual contract value, which was an increase of 130% YoY. There are two reasons that NOW is selling off, in my opinion. First was the 75 bps Middle East headwind on subscription growth and Armis integration margin drag. Due to the narrative about AI displacing and replacing SaaS companies, NOW needed to definitively shatter the consensus estimates on the top and bottom line, which didn’t happen. I think the market is getting the narrative wrong, and while NOW previously traded at a lofty multiple, this is no longer the story, and the pendulum has swung too far in the opposite direction. I am bullish on NOW after going through Q1 earnings and think that shares are cheap after getting clobbered after a print that should have had the opposite effect.

The Q1 print for ServiceNow was strong, and the reaction after hours is wrong

NOW generated $3.77 billion in revenue, which was an increase of 22% YoY and a beat on the $3.74 billion consensus number. NOW generated $3.67 billion in subscription revenue, which was an increase of 22% YoY and exceeded NOW’s guidance of $3.65 billion. NOW’s adjusted EPS came in at $0.97, which was in line with estimates. The non-GAAP operating income was $1.2 billion, which was a 31.8% margin, as it beat the consensus number of $1.18 billion and NOW’s own guide of 31.5%. The current remaining performance obligations for NOW came in at $12.64 billion, which increased 22.5% YoY, and their total RPO increased 25% YoY to $27.7 billion. I believe that the RPO number tells an incredible story because NOW has $27.7 billion of signed contract revenue sitting in backlog that has not been reflected in their numbers yet. The backlog is nearly 2 years of current annualized revenue, and it is compounding at a faster rate than the reported top line. This metric indicates that this is not a business rolling over or being disrupted by AI. The customer cohort data tells the same story. NOW closed 16 transactions worth more than $5 million in new annual contract value in Q1, which was roughly an 80% YoY increase. NOW has 630 customers with more than $5 million in annual contract value. Large enterprises are not pulling back on NOW’s services, and these are not contracts that can be ended out of the blue.

The more important part of the report is what management did with the 2026 guide. Coming into the print NOW had guided for full-year subscription revenue of $15.53-$15.57 billion. The range was increased to $15.74 to $15.78 billion. That is roughly a $210 million bump at the midpoint, and it implies that NOW’s subscription revenue will grow in excess of 22%. That is not the only number that moved, as management also raised full-year guidance for subscription gross profit margin, non-GAAP operating margin, and free cash flow (FCF) margin. The current framework calls for a 32% operating margin and a 36% FCF margin as revenue scales more than 20%. What should have given the market some reprieve is that the CFO commentary included an assessment of the geopolitical environment for the remainder of the year that was taken into consideration for the guidance increase. The Middle East headwind is already baked into the new number. NOW produced strong enough Q1 earnings that it was able to absorb the hit while leading to a full-year guidance increase.

I think that the reaction is wrong, and the tape did what the tape has been doing NOW all year. The clean beat and raise got punished, and shares of NOW fell below $90 at one point in after-hours trading. NOW is in a bear market, as they have fallen more than -50% from the 52-week high of $211.48. At some point, you have to separate the fundamentals from the sentiment, and Q1 earnings should have put us in that position. I think we are getting to this point, and I believe that the market is mispricing one of the highest-quality enterprise software businesses. There is still a misunderstanding about what can be vibe coded away and what can't, which may make the current landscape seem scary but also could generate a tremendous buying opportunity for investors who can stomach volatility.

The AI Disruption Thesis Is Failing and Now Assist Is Working

The single most important narrative weighing on NOW has been the SaaSpocalypse thesis. The idea that agentic AI will make horizontal workflow software obsolete is not reflected in NOW’s numbers. Now Assist customers are spending over $1 million in annual contract value, and NOW’s AI revenue run rate is now $1.5 billion, which was up 50% YoY and ahead of their original $1 billion target for 2026. They launched Autonomous Workforce during Q1, which is a new class of AI specialists designed to execute enterprise jobs end-to-end with built-in governance and human oversight. The first out-of-the-box offering is a Level 1 Service Desk AI Specialist that autonomously diagnoses and resolves common IT support requests. NOW also launched EmployeeWorks, which is a front door for the enterprise that combines Moveworks AI and enterprise search with NOW’s autonomous workflows to convert plain language requests into completed actions.

If the AI disruption thesis were correct, enterprise customers would be pausing or cutting their spend with NOW while they figure out what agentic AI means for them. Instead, these enterprises are doubling and tripling down. The $1 million-plus ACV cohort on Now Assist is the cohort that has the most at stake in getting AI right. If these companies were worried NOW was about to be disrupted or if their IT departments were vibe coding better software, they would not be increasing their spending with NOW. The partnership ecosystem is also expanding as it advanced its NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) partnership at NVIDIA GTC in March with its Autonomous Workforce designed to leverage NVDA’s latest AI infrastructure. At Google Cloud Next, NOW deepened its Google Cloud partnership with new AI solutions and agents for autonomous enterprise operations. NOW has also announced relationships with Microsoft (MSFT), Anthropic, and OpenAI as it positions itself at the center of how large enterprises deploy AI.

NOW’s valuation is looking too cheap to ignore

When I look at the consensus estimates for NOW, they are expected to generate $4.19 of EPS in 2026, which places them at 23.90 times earnings. This is a growth rate of 19.35% YoY, and NOW is expected to generate $5.02 of EPS in 2027 and $6.12 in 2027. NOW is expected to exceed a 19% YoY growth rate over the next 3 years on EPS and trades at 16.36 times 2028 earnings. This is very inexpensive for a company growing this quickly. NOW is also growing at similar growth rates on the top line. NOW is expected to increase revenue by 20.41% YoY in 2026, then by 18.44% in 2027 and 18.48% in 2028. NOW is expected to generate $15.99 billion in revenue in 2026 and $22.44 billion in 2028. These are not the metrics of a company being vibe coded away or seeing sales displaced due to AI. It looks like they are benefiting from AI instead.

While I am bullish on NOW, there are risks to consider.

I want to be fair about the bear case because the stock is down for reasons that are not entirely imaginary. There is an AI disruption overhang for NOW. If agentic AI tooling genuinely does start to eat into horizontal workflow software demand over the next 18-24 months, we could see SaaS multiples be rerated lower. NOW has acquired Moveworks, Armis, and Veza, which could negatively impact NOW if they are not integrated correctly. If the margin trajectory does not normalize in 2027, the bull thesis from adding these companies could become a bear talking point. If sentiment on the software sector continues to deteriorate, the sector could get rerated to the downside, and NOW will be impacted. Investors should do their own due diligence on NOW, as this is an investment that is down more than -50% peak to trough.

Conclusion

I believe that NOW reported a Q1 print that, on a pure fundamentals basis, should have driven the stock higher. They beat on the top line, raised guidance, and delivered 130%+ growth in $1M-plus Now Assist customers. NOW also generated a $1.5 billion AI revenue run rate, which should have dismantled the SaaSpocalypse narrative. NOW repurchased 20.1 million shares, which is more than what they repurchased in 2025, yet shares still fell below $90 at one point after hours. I believe that the disconnect is an opportunity, and the post-earnings weakness won't last forever. NOW has $27.7 billion in RPO and trades at an aggressive multiple for its growth metrics. Shares could go lower, but I think this is a great price point for long-term investors who can handle volatility and an unpopular narrative.

data.title: Ecolab: Cool Off With Heating Up Returns
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

So often, I find that investing behind the scenes is more successful for my Investment Group, Dividend Kings, and me than investing directly into the headline.

Many great traders find success trading in and out of names that are impacted by headlines that come across their computer screens or phone screens. They will jump in and out of potential opportunities, often buying rumors and selling the news in an attempt to generate a profit. This is extremely risky and time-consuming.

For many investors, especially those who are near retirement or in retirement, they don't want to be beholden to their cell phone and every piece of news that comes across it and constantly have to decide when the right time is to buy and sell. Instead, they are more interested in a portfolio that reliably provides them with what they're looking for: strong returns over the long run with minimal direct effort having to be put in. The way that I find to do this is to not buy what's up front, but to buy what's behind the scenes.

For example, we all know that data centers are being developed en masse in multiple places around the globe because of the need for AI, computing power, the Internet of Things, and to store massive amounts of data that are gathered regularly. I've highlighted before opportunities to buy the landowners of data centers, who then rent out the properties. I've highlighted various utilities that will be providing the power to these data centers, and today I want to look at another behind-the-scenes opportunity that ties into AI and data centers that have nothing to do with the programmers or the programs themselves.

Let's dive in!

A Dividend Aristocrat Solving Water Scarcity in the AI Era

Clean water, safe food, and healthy environments are just a few of the things that many of us take for granted. Ecolab (ECL) plays a vital role in making that possible for us. The company’s approximately 48,000 associates in over 170 countries serve almost 3 million customer locations, providing the essential hygiene and water management infrastructure that allows modern industry to function.

For a better idea of how critical ECL is to the world, here are a few impressive stats on it from 2025:

  • The company protects over one-third (36%) of the world’s processed food production.
  • ECL also helped to protect 1.7 billion people from foodborne illnesses and infections.
  • It also supported the generation of almost one-quarter (22%) of the world’s power.
  • Finally, ECL conserved enough water to meet the drinking needs of 781 million people.

The company’s appeal lies in the fact that its business model is built on a recurring revenue model. They provide integrated systems, such as sensors, dispensers, and digital monitoring, that help customers minimize water and energy use while achieving optimal hygiene. Once an ECL system is installed in a facility, they continue purchasing consumables and services to keep it running.

The most significant new catalyst for ECL has to do with the AI and data center cooling boom. As AI computing expands, it will require more of the sophisticated cooling and ultrapure water systems that ECL sells. The company’s acquisition of Ovivo Electronics, completed last December, more than doubled its capacity in the microelectronics space. ECL now offers site-to-chip solutions that protect servers using real-time coolant health monitoring.

Water scarcity also remains a huge problem for the world, with global demand forecasted to exceed supply by about 56% by 2030. As water becomes more expensive and regulated, this will only raise the demand from industrial giants for ECL’s water recycling and reuse technologies.

As I alluded to earlier, another key component of the company’s growth has to do with its bolt-on acquisitions. The latest acquisition was a $4.75 billion deal for CoolIT Systems, which is a global leader in advanced liquid cooling for next-gen AI data centers. The rapidly growing $550 million sales base over the next 12 months is expected to accelerate ECL’s total organic sales growth rate by 1%.

Acquisitions like these are made possible by the company’s A- S&P credit rating, which affords it a low cost of capital. This is why the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for adjusted diluted EPS to compound by 12.7% annually through 2028, off a 2025 base of $7.53.

With its next quarterly earnings release expected to come out on April 28, analysts predict ECL will report earnings of around $1.7 per share. This is sequentially lower than last quarter, which was the final quarter of its fiscal year. ECL has a level of expected seasonality. FY1 is historically the lowest-earning performance quarter. In a year-over-year comparison, the expectation of $1.7 EPS is a step up from last year's FY1 performance of $1.50 per share. This estimate falls right into the predicted range from ECL's own management. On March 23, they provided slides regarding their merger with CoolIT Systems and expected that their first quarter results would be between $1.69 and $1.71. Analysts essentially just cut that right in the middle with their production.

ECL’s 1.1% dividend yield is about in line with the S&P 500 index’s (SP500) 1.2% yield. The company’s dividend is secure, with its payout ratio set to be in the mid-30% range in 2026. That allows it the flexibility to extend its 34-year dividend growth streak with payout growth roughly matching adjusted diluted EPS growth (for a Chowder number of around 13 to 14).

ECL is what we consider a wonderful business for a fair price. At the current $275.68 share price (as of April 21, 2026), shares are priced at a forward 12-month PE ratio of 32.6. According to data from FAST Graphs, this is far less than the 10-year average PE ratio of 34.1.

Looking ahead, we think that a fair value multiple of 32 is reasonable for ECL. The company’s annual forward adjusted diluted EPS growth consensus is 12.7%. For more perspective, that’s well above the 10-year CAGR of 5.6%. This points to accelerating growth prospects for ECL. Combine that with its Dividend Aristocrat status, and this is what supports our fair value PE ratio. That suggests shares are trading at a discount to our fair value per share estimate of $283.

If ECL meets growth expectations and reverts to fair value, its near-term total returns through March 2027 would be modest at 3%. By the end of 2031, though, the company could have a clear path to 11%+ annual total returns.

ECL does have risks that it must navigate, however. Its key risks include energy/raw material inflation and currency/geopolitical headwinds.

Last month, the company announced a global 10% to 14% energy surcharge that went into effect at the start of this month. The spike in crude oil prices and natural gas prices is meaningfully raising ECL’s input costs, which presents two risks here. If these surcharges fail to fully cover the rise in input costs, its operating margins could contract. If customers push back on these surcharges, that could soften demand.

Given ECL’s significant operations in Europe and the Middle East, it is vulnerable to the strengthening U.S. dollar and regional instability. These could cause actual results to materially differ from their 2026 adjusted diluted EPS guidance.

Conclusion

Instead of trying to determine which AI program will be predominant or which startup will provide the best massive returns. You can find reliable, dependable, and excellent returns by investing in the companies that provide services to all the players in the game. ECL provides the necessary cooling needs for data centers, regardless of who owns the servers there. By being a provider of essential services to burgeoning and growing sectors, you can enjoy strong returns for decades to come. To do this, though, you must look beyond the headlines and into the background.

When building a financial fortress, you need to have a portfolio that isn't there to be exciting in the present. That doesn't mean your portfolio should be boring to you and you should have no interest in it, but it means that you need to have a portfolio that is going to provide you with stability and returns over the long run. This can often be achieved more readily by investing in companies that are behind the scenes, providing essential services instead of the companies that are getting all the headline drama and noise. By stepping away from the chaos, you can find clarity just like we do in Dividend Kings every single day.

data.title: Airbus: Orders Explode, But Delivery Crisis Deepens
data.description:
European jet maker Airbus (EADSF)(EADSY) seemingly was positioned to benefit substantially from the crisis that unfolded at its competitor Boeing since 2018. However, what we have seen is that Airbus had an extremely difficult time hiking production to its desired production rates as supply chains have been in a challenging spot coming out of the pandemic, and the issues with the GTF engines affecting the A320neo and A220 have not made things easier for the European jet maker. Airbus has a backlog of over 9,000 airplanes, so collecting orders has not been the problem; delivering those airplanes has been. That makes it interesting to assess the company’s monthly deliveries and orders.

In this report, I analyze the airplane orders and deliveries for March 2026. Based on a single month, no conclusions can be drawn, but looking at orders and deliveries month by month and in the year-to-date frame provides a good view of consistency in deliveries, as we are not so much worried about orders for the foreseeable future.

Airbus Order Inflow Surges With Over 330 New Orders

For this analysis, I'm using a data visualization and intelligence tool developed by The Aerospace Forum, aiming to make the complexities of the commercial airplane manufacturing industry more understandable.

In March, Airbus booked a total of 331 orders valued at $23.2 billion. Single-aisle orders accounted for 85% of all orders with 9% of the orders for wide-body airplanes and the remaining 6% attributed to freighter orders.

  • Delta Air Lines ordered 34 A321neo aircraft and additionally ordered 16 A330-900 aircraft and 15 A350-900 aircraft.
  • NAS Aviation Services ordered 23 A320neo aircraft and 77 A321neo aircraft.
  • Juneyao Air ordered 13 A320neo aircraft and 12 A321neo aircraft.
  • China Eastern Airlines ordered 18 A320neo aircraft and 83 A321neo aircraft.
  • Atlas Air ordered 20 A350F aircraft.
  • Undisclosed customers ordered 20 A220-300 aircraft.

During the month, the following order book changes and alterations took place:

  • Frontier Airlines took over two A320neo aircraft from BOC Aviation.
  • CMB Financial Leasing was identified as the customer for one A320neo aircraft.
  • ICBC Leasing was identified as the customer for one A320neo aircraft and one A321neo aircraft.
  • KLM Royal Dutch Airlines was identified as the customer for two A321neo aircraft.
  • Qingdao Airlines was identified as the customer for one A321neo aircraft.
  • Odyssey Airlines cancelled an order for ten A220-100 aircraft.

In terms of order inflow, Airbus had a good month, with the A320neo family showing its value for Airbus and customers, and there also were several wide body and freighter orders. After subtracting 10 cancellations, the jet maker was left with 321 net orders valued at $22.9 billion. In the same month last year, Airbus booked 139 net orders valued at $10.2 billion. So, we saw a significant increase in the orders driven by lower cancellation activity and big orders from lessors and Delta Air Lines (DAL).

Year-to-date, the company has now booked a total of 398 net orders valued at $27.2 billion compared to 204 net orders in the first three months of 2025 valued at $16.5 billion. While Airbus is not executing its production ramp-up as planned and there are macro concerns, we see that there is no softening in order inflow. That is not fully surprising given that airplanes are ordered with a long-term fleet plan in mind.

Airbus Aircraft Deliveries Still Do Not Look Great

In March, Airbus delivered 60 airplanes, including 49 single-aisle and 11 wide-body airplanes valued at $4.2 billion:

  • The A220 family saw deliveries of eight aircraft, including one A220-100 aircraft and seven A220-300 aircraft.
  • The A320 family saw deliveries of fifty-three aircraft, including one A319neo aircraft, twenty-two A320neo aircraft, and thirty A321neo aircraft.
  • The A330 family saw deliveries of three aircraft, all A330-900 aircraft.
  • The A350 family saw deliveries of nine aircraft, all A350-900 aircraft.

Sequentially, Airbus delivered 25 more airplanes compared to February, adding around $2.1 billion in delivery value. For the first three months of the year, Airbus delivered 114 airplanes valued at $7.4 billion. A year ago, Airbus delivered 136 airplanes in the first three months valued at $8.4 billion. A220 deliveries increased by two units, but A320neo family deliveries declined by 25 units, while A330neo deliveries declined by one unit and A350 deliveries increased by two units.

For 2026, Airbus is targeting 870 airplane deliveries, targeting a 10% year-on-year increase. However, in the first three months we are seeing a 16% decline, and that points at a challenging delivery schedule for Airbus. The European jet maker is used to having a backloaded delivery schedule, but the question is whether the company has not set a too ambitious delivery target once again. Typically, around this time, the deliveries should be around 15-20 percent of the full-year delivery figure. So, Airbus’ current pattern is suggesting that it would not get more than 665 deliveries at the mid-point.

The book-to-bill ratio during March was 5.5x in terms of units and value, reflecting weak delivery numbers. For the first three months, the book-to-bill ratio is 3.6x in terms of units and 3.7x in terms of dollar value.

Conclusion: Airbus Performance Is Underwhelming

Airbus continues to book orders, and in March we saw a big bump in the order tally. However, the company’s delivery volumes are underwhelming. We did see a usual end-of-quarter increase in the deliveries, but Airbus needs to deliver over 80 airplanes on average in the balance of the year, and that is a steep increase for a company that averaged only 38 deliveries in Q1. I maintain a bullish view on Airbus due to the big backlog, but in terms of execution, the company seems to be unable to streamline delivery flows and has been counting on end-of-year surges driven by supply chain bottlenecks easing for several years now.
